2026-03-31T03:23:16.7505340Z 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7505556Z Agent: Good, I can see the issues:
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7506040Z Agent: 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7508659Z Agent: 1. **`ProxyResource_1`** - A naming collision issue where TypeSpec generated `ProxyResource_1` as a fallback name due to a name collision. The `ProxyResource_1` is a different interface from `ProxyResource extends Resource` - it doesn't have `systemData`. Both `AlertModification` and `AlertsSummary` extend `ProxyResource_1` instead of `ProxyResource`.
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7511310Z Agent: 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7513181Z Agent: 2. **`AzureClouds` enum** - Uses UPPER_SNAKE_CASE members (`AZURE_CHINA_CLOUD`, `AZURE_PUBLIC_CLOUD`, `AZURE_US_GOVERNMENT`) instead of PascalCase, AND doesn't have the `Known` prefix. This violates the naming convention: enums should be named `KnownXxx` with PascalCase values.
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7515139Z Agent:    
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7516397Z Agent: 3. **`AzureSupportedClouds`** uses a closed template literal type instead of `string`, which forces users to import the enum to use these values. That's not ideal for API usability.
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7519162Z Agent: 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7520091Z Agent: 4. `KnownTimeRange` looks good now with the proper PascalCase naming - the _N prefix issues from the previous review seem to have been fixed.

2026-03-31T03:23:16.7557101Z Agent: Now I have a clear picture. Let me assess the issues in PR #37870:
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7557766Z Agent: 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7558101Z Agent: **Issues Found:**
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7558498Z Agent: 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7561627Z Agent: 1. **`ProxyResource_1`** (line 618 of arm-alertsmanagement-node.api.md) — This is a `_N` suffix which is a naming violation. The guideline says: "Avoid `_N` suffixes, for example both `Resource` and `Resource_1` interfaces. This usually means duplicated models in TypeSpec; suggest using `@clientName` to rename one or merging them." Both `AlertModification` (line 69) and `AlertsSummary` (line 247) extend `ProxyResource_1`. 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7564109Z Agent: 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7564474Z Agent: This same naming issue came up in PR #37633.
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7564971Z Agent: 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7566748Z Agent: 2. **`AzureClouds` enum** (line 268) — Uses UPPER_SNAKE_CASE members (`AZURE_CHINA_CLOUD`, `AZURE_PUBLIC_CLOUD`, `AZURE_US_GOVERNMENT`) instead of PascalCase. The guidelines don't explicitly call this out, but it's part of a broader pattern we should address.
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7568682Z Agent: 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7569829Z Agent: Looking back at the previous PR, the `KnownTimeRange` enum's `_N` prefix issue appears to be resolved now, but `ProxyResource_1` with its `_N` suffix collision is still present.
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7571098Z Agent: 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7572251Z Agent: 3. **`AzureSupportedClouds`** (line 275) — This uses a closed template literal type based on `AzureClouds` instead of just accepting `string`.
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7573340Z Agent: 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7576108Z Agent: I've flagged this same pattern in earlier PRs like #37572 and #37369, but I'm realizing the guidelines don't actually have an explicit rule against template literal types. They mention avoiding `unknown` and `void` return types, but nothing about template literal types specifically. I should stick to what's documented in the guidelines rather than applying patterns from previous reviews that might not have a basis in the actual rules.
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7579101Z Agent: 
2026-03-31T03:23:16.7580966Z Agent: So let me focus on what I can actually justify: `ProxyResource_1` clearly violates the `_N` suffix rule, so that's worth flagging as a spec design issue. The `AzureClouds` enum with UPPER_SNAKE_CASE values isn't explicitly covered in the guidelines either, and I need to check whether it's missing the `Known` prefix before deciding if that's a real issue. I'm checking whether the enum naming conventions actually ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2389 chars]

2026-03-31T03:23:16.7598700Z Agent: I'm noticing the `ProxyResource_1` naming issue—we have two interfaces where `ProxyResource` extends `Resource` with a systemData field, while `ProxyResource_1` is a separate interface without it. As for the `AzureClouds` enum, while it doesn't follow the `Known` prefix pattern seen in other enums, I'm not finding an explicit guideline that requires it, since this enum represents cloud types rather than following the standard `KnownXxx` pattern. Looking at the enum members with their UPPER_SNAKE_CASE values, I need to determine if there's a specific issue to flag here. I'm checking the naming conventions against the guidelines — `ProxyResource_1` violates the rule against `_N` suffixes, so that's a clear issue. The UPPER_SNAKE_CASE pattern in `AzureClouds` and the template literal type approach don't match any explicit guidelines, so I'll skip those. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.\n 5. get_review_comments - Get review threads on a pull request. Each thread contains logically grouped review comments made on the same code location during pull request reviews. file\"},{\"filename\":\"sdk/alertsmanagement/arm-alertsmanagement/README.md\",\"status\":\"added\",\"additions\":113,\"changes\":113,\"patch\":\"@@ -0,0 +1,113 @@\\n+# Azure AlertsManagement client library for JavaScript\\n+\\n+This package contains an isomorphic SDK (runs both in Node.js and in browsers) for Azure AlertsManagement client.\\n+\\n+Azure Alerts Management Service provides a single pane of glass of alerts across Azure Monitor.\\n+\\n+Key links:\\n+\\n+- [Source code](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/tree/main/sdk/alertsmanagement/arm-alertsmanagement)\\n+- [Package (NPM)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@azure/arm-alertsmanagement)\\n+- [API reference documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/javascript/api/@azure/arm-alertsmanagement?view=azure-node-preview)\\n+- [Samples](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/tree/main/sdk/alertsmanagement/arm-alertsmanagement/samples)\\n+\\n+## Getting started\\n+\\n+### Currently supported environments\\n+\\n+- [LTS versions of Node.js](https://github.com/nodejs/release#release-schedule)\\n+- Latest versions of Safari, Chrome, Edge and Firefox.\\n+\\n+See our [support policy](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/blob/main/SUPPORT.md) for more details.\\n+\\n+### Prerequisites\\n+\\n+- An [Azure subscription][azure_sub].\\n+\\n+### Install the `@azure/arm-alertsmanagement` package\\n+\\n+Install the Azure AlertsManagement client library for JavaScript with `npm`:\\n+\\n+```bash\\n+npm install @azure/arm-alertsmanagement\\n+```\\n+\\n+### Create and authenticate a `AlertsManagementClient`\\n+\\n+To create a client object to access the Azure AlertsManagement API, you will need the `endpoint` of your Azure AlertsManagement resource and a `credential`. The Azure AlertsManagement client can use Azure Active Directory credentials to authenticate.\\n+You can find the endpoint for your Azure AlertsManagement resource in the [Azure Portal][azure_portal].\\n+\\n+You can authenticate with Azure Active Directory using a credential from the [@azure/identity][azure_identity] library or [an existing AAD Token](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/blob/main/sdk/identity/identity/samples/AzureIdentityExamples.md#authenticating-with-a-pre-fetched-access-token).\\n+\\n+To use the [DefaultAzureCredential][defaultazurecredential] provider shown below, or other credential providers provided with the Azure SDK, please install the `@azure/identity` package:\\n+\\n+```bash\\n+npm install @azure/identity\\n+```\\n+\\n+You will also need to **register a new AAD application and grant access to Azure AlertsManagement** by assigning the suitable role to your service principal (note: roles such as `\\\"Owner\\\"` will not grant the necessary permissions).\\n+\\n+For more information about how to create an Azure AD Application check out [this guide](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/active-directory/develop/howto-create-service-principal-portal).\\n+\\n+Using Node.js and Node-like environments, you can use the `DefaultAzureCredential` class to authenticate the client.\\n+\\n+```ts snippet:ReadmeSampleCreateClient_Node\\n+import { AlertsManagementClient } from \\\"@azure/arm-alertsmanagement\\\";\\n+import { DefaultAzureCredential } from \\\"@azure/identity\\\";\\n+\\n+const client = new AlertsManagementClient(new DefaultAzureCredential());\\n+```\\n+\\n+For browser environments, use the `InteractiveBrowserCredential` from the `@azure/identity` package to authenticate.\\n+\\n+```ts snippet:ReadmeSampleCreateClient_Browser\\n+import { InteractiveBrowserCredential } from \\\"@azure/identity\\\";\\n+import { AlertsManagementClient } from \\\"@azure/arm-alertsmanagement\\\";\\n+\\n+const credential = new InteractiveBrowserCredential({\\n+  tenantId: \\\"\\u003cYOUR_TENANT_ID\\u003e\\\",\\n+  clientId: \\\"\\u003cYOUR_CLIENT_ID\\u003e\\\",\\n+});\\n+const client = new AlertsManagementClient(credential);\\n+```\\n+\\n+\\n+### JavaScript Bundle\\n+To use this client library in the browser, first you need to use a bundler. For details on how to do this, please refer to our [bundling documentation](https://aka.ms/AzureSDKBundling).\\n+\\n+## Key concepts\\n+\\n+### AlertsManagementClient\\n+\\n+`AlertsManagementClient` is the primary interface for developers using the Azure AlertsManagement client library. Explore the methods on this client object to understand the different features of the Azure AlertsManagement service that you can access.\\n+\\n+## Troubleshooting\\n+\\n+### Logging\\n+\\n+Enabling logging may help uncover useful information about failures. In order to see a log of HTTP requests and responses, set the `AZURE_LOG_LEVEL` environment variable to `info`. Alternatively, logging can be enabled at runtime by calling `setLogLevel` in the `@azure/logger`:\\n+\\n+```ts snippet:SetLogLevel\\n+import { setLogLevel } from \\\"@azure/logger\\\";\\n+\\n+setLogLevel(\\\"info\\\");\\n+```\\n+\\n+For more detailed instructions on how to enable logs, you can look at the [@azure/logger package docs](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/tree/main/sdk/core/logger).\\n+\\n+## Next steps\\n+\\n+Please take a look at the [samples](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/tree/main/sdk/alertsmanagement/arm-alertsmanagement/samples) directory for detailed examples on how to use this library.\\n+\\n+## Contributing\\n+\\n+If you'd like to contribute to this library, please read the [contributing guide](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) to learn more about how to build and test the code.\\n+\\n+## Related projects\\n+\\n+- [Microsoft Azure SDK for JavaScript](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js)\\n+\\n+[azure_sub]: https://azure.microsoft.com/free/\\n+[azure_portal]: https://portal.azure.com\\n+[azure_identity]: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/tree/main/sdk/identity/identity\\n+[defaultazurecredential]: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/tree/main/sdk/identity/identity#defaultazurecredential\"},{\"filename\":\"sdk/alertsmanagement/arm-alertsmanagement/api-extractor.json\",\"status\":\"added\",\"additions\":1,\"changes\":1,\"patch\":\"@@ -0,0 +1 @@\\n+{ \\\"extends\\\": \\\"../../../api-extractor-base.json\\\" }\"},{\"filename\":\"sdk/alertsmanagement/arm-alertsmanagement/eslint.config.mjs\",\"status\":\"added\",\"additions\":14,\"changes\":14,\"patch\":\"@@ -0,0 +1,14 @@\\n+import azsdkEslint from \\\"@azure/eslint-plugin-azure-sdk\\\";\\n+\\n+export default azsdkEslint.config([\\n+  {\\n+    rules: {\\n+      \\\"@azure/azure-sdk/ts-modules-only-named\\\": \\\"warn\\\",\\n+      \\\"@azure/azure-sdk/ts-package-json-type
